stem: [Endocrine] A client reports new/worsening symptoms related to Endocrine. Which nursing action is the priority?
choices: Ask family members what they think should be done
Document the complaint and reassess at the next scheduled round
Assess airway/breathing/circulation and escalate per protocol if unstable
Provide detailed teaching first to reduce anxiety
answer: Assess airway/breathing/circulation and escalate per protocol if unstable
rationale: Prioritize ABCs and rapid escalation for potentially unstable presentations.

stem: [Endocrine] Which finding most strongly indicates a need for immediate provider notification in a client with Endocrine-related concerns?
choices: Rapidly worsening vital signs or acute change in level of consciousness
A long-standing symptom that is unchanged from baseline
Mild discomfort that improves with rest
Stable vital signs with normal mentation
answer: Rapidly worsening vital signs or acute change in level of consciousness
rationale: Acute deterioration (vital signs/LOC) is a red flag requiring urgent escalation.

stem: [Endocrine] The nurse is providing discharge teaching on Endocrine. Which statement by the client shows correct understanding?
choices: I will change doses based on how I feel without contacting my provider
I will follow the plan consistently and seek help early if symptoms worsen
I will avoid follow-up visits unless I have severe pain
I will stop treatment as soon as I feel better
answer: I will follow the plan consistently and seek help early if symptoms worsen
rationale: Adherence and early follow-up reduce complications and relapse.

stem: [Endocrine] A client is at risk for complications related to Endocrine. Which assessment is most important to trend?
choices: Hair distribution and nail length
Daily television viewing time
Favorite foods and hobbies
Focused assessment plus objective measures (vitals, I&O, labs) relevant to the condition
answer: Focused assessment plus objective measures (vitals, I&O, labs) relevant to the condition
rationale: Trends in objective findings detect early deterioration and guide interventions.

stem: [Endocrine] Which intervention best supports patient safety when caring for a client with Endocrine concerns?
choices: Encourage the client to ambulate alone immediately regardless of status
Use room number as the only identifier before medications
Use standard precautions and implement condition-specific safety measures
Skip reassessment after interventions to reduce interruptions
answer: Use standard precautions and implement condition-specific safety measures
rationale: Safety includes infection control, reassessment, fall prevention, and correct identification.

stem: [Endocrine] A client asks why a specific symptom occurs in Endocrine. Which explanation is most appropriate?
choices: It always means the patient is exaggerating
It reflects the underlying mechanism of the condition and the body’s compensatory response
It is caused only by dehydration in all cases
It happens randomly and cannot be explained
answer: It reflects the underlying mechanism of the condition and the body’s compensatory response
rationale: Teaching should connect symptoms to mechanism and compensation in a clear, nonjudgmental way.

stem: [Endocrine] Which delegation is appropriate when managing care related to Endocrine?
choices: Delegate evaluation of response to a new IV medication to UAP
Delegate medication teaching to UAP
Delegate initial assessment of new chest pain to UAP
Delegate routine, stable tasks (e.g., vitals) to UAP and keep assessment/teaching/evaluation with the nurse
answer: Delegate routine, stable tasks (e.g., vitals) to UAP and keep assessment/teaching/evaluation with the nurse
rationale: Delegation must match scope: UAP can do routine tasks; nurse retains assessment/teaching/evaluation.

stem: [Endocrine] The nurse suspects medication-related complication affecting Endocrine. What is the best first step?
choices: Stop all medications without notifying anyone
Hold the suspected med if unsafe per parameters, assess, and notify provider per policy
Give an extra dose to overcome the problem
Ignore the finding if the client is not complaining
answer: Hold the suspected med if unsafe per parameters, assess, and notify provider per policy
rationale: Safety first: assess, follow parameters, and communicate changes promptly.

stem: [Endocrine] Which documentation is most appropriate for a Endocrine-related event?
choices: The patient was being difficult and noncompliant
The patient is dramatic and exaggerating
The caregiver is obviously lying
Objective findings with measurements and direct quotes
answer: Objective findings with measurements and direct quotes
rationale: Document objectively; avoid labels and conclusions.

stem: [Endocrine] A client becomes anxious while discussing Endocrine. Which nurse response is most therapeutic?
choices: Let’s not talk about it
Use calm presence and ask an open-ended question to explore concerns
Don’t worry—you’ll be fine
You shouldn’t feel that way
answer: Use calm presence and ask an open-ended question to explore concerns
rationale: Therapeutic communication uses empathy, calm presence, and open-ended questions.

Don’t worry—you’ll be fine
You shouldn’t feel that way
Use calm presence and ask an open-ended question to explore concerns
answer: Use calm presence and ask an open-ended question to explore concerns
rationale: Therapeutic communication uses empathy, calm presence, and open-ended questions.

stem: [Safety & Infect Control] A client reports new/worsening symptoms related to Safety & Infect Control. Which nursing action is the priority?
choices: Ask family members what they think should be done
Document the complaint and reassess at the next scheduled round
Provide detailed teaching first to reduce anxiety
Assess airway/breathing/circulation and escalate per protocol if unstable
answer: Assess airway/breathing/circulation and escalate per protocol if unstable
rationale: Prioritize ABCs and rapid escalation for potentially unstable presentations.

stem: [Safety & Infect Control] Which finding most strongly indicates a need for immediate provider notification in a client with Safety & Infect Control-related concerns?
choices: A long-standing symptom that is unchanged from baseline
Mild discomfort that improves with rest
Rapidly worsening vital signs or acute change in level of consciousness
Stable vital signs with normal mentation
answer: Rapidly worsening vital signs or acute change in level of consciousness
rationale: Acute deterioration (vital signs/LOC) is a red flag requiring urgent escalation.

stem: [Safety & Infect Control] The nurse is providing discharge teaching on Safety & Infect Control. Which statement by the client shows correct understanding?
choices: I will avoid follow-up visits unless I have severe pain
I will stop treatment as soon as I feel better
I will change doses based on how I feel without contacting my provider
I will follow the plan consistently and seek help early if symptoms worsen
answer: I will follow the plan consistently and seek help early if symptoms worsen
rationale: Adherence and early follow-up reduce complications and relapse.

stem: [Safety & Infect Control] A client is at risk for complications related to Safety & Infect Control. Which assessment is most important to trend?
choices: Daily television viewing time
Focused assessment plus objective measures (vitals, I&O, labs) relevant to the condition
Favorite foods and hobbies
Hair distribution and nail length
answer: Focused assessment plus objective measures (vitals, I&O, labs) relevant to the condition
rationale: Trends in objective findings detect early deterioration and guide interventions.

stem: [Safety & Infect Control] Which intervention best supports patient safety when caring for a client with Safety & Infect Control concerns?
choices: Encourage the client to ambulate alone immediately regardless of status
Skip reassessment after interventions to reduce interruptions
Use room number as the only identifier before medications
Use standard precautions and implement condition-specific safety measures
answer: Use standard precautions and implement condition-specific safety measures
rationale: Safety includes infection control, reassessment, fall prevention, and correct identification.

stem: [Safety & Infect Control] A client asks why a specific symptom occurs in Safety & Infect Control. Which explanation is most appropriate?
choices: It always means the patient is exaggerating
It is caused only by dehydration in all cases
It reflects the underlying mechanism of the condition and the body’s compensatory response
It happens randomly and cannot be explained
answer: It reflects the underlying mechanism of the condition and the body’s compensatory response
rationale: Teaching should connect symptoms to mechanism and compensation in a clear, nonjudgmental way.

stem: [Safety & Infect Control] Which delegation is appropriate when managing care related to Safety & Infect Control?
choices: Delegate evaluation of response to a new IV medication to UAP
Delegate medication teaching to UAP
Delegate initial assessment of new chest pain to UAP
Delegate routine, stable tasks (e.g., vitals) to UAP and keep assessment/teaching/evaluation with the nurse
answer: Delegate routine, stable tasks (e.g., vitals) to UAP and keep assessment/teaching/evaluation with the nurse
rationale: Delegation must match scope: UAP can do routine tasks; nurse retains assessment/teaching/evaluation.

stem: [Safety & Infect Control] The nurse suspects medication-related complication affecting Safety & Infect Control. What is the best first step?
choices: Stop all medications without notifying anyone
Hold the suspected med if unsafe per parameters, assess, and notify provider per policy
Give an extra dose to overcome the problem
Ignore the finding if the client is not complaining
answer: Hold the suspected med if unsafe per parameters, assess, and notify provider per policy
rationale: Safety first: assess, follow parameters, and communicate changes promptly.

stem: [Safety & Infect Control] Which documentation is most appropriate for a Safety & Infect Control-related event?
choices: Objective findings with measurements and direct quotes
The patient was being difficult and noncompliant
The patient is dramatic and exaggerating
The caregiver is obviously lying
answer: Objective findings with measurements and direct quotes
rationale: Document objectively; avoid labels and conclusions.

answer: Use calm presence and ask an open-ended question to explore concerns

Use calm presence and ask an open-ended question to explore concerns
Don’t worry—you’ll be fine
Let’s not talk about it
answer: Use calm presence and ask an open-ended question to explore concerns
rationale: Therapeutic communication uses empathy, calm presence, and open-ended questions.

stem: [Palliation] A client reports new/worsening symptoms related to Palliation. Which nursing action is the priority?
choices: Assess airway/breathing/circulation and escalate per protocol if unstable
Provide detailed teaching first to reduce anxiety
Ask family members what they think should be done
Document the complaint and reassess at the next scheduled round
answer: Assess airway/breathing/circulation and escalate per protocol if unstable
rationale: Prioritize ABCs and rapid escalation for potentially unstable presentations.

stem: [Palliation] Which finding most strongly indicates a need for immediate provider notification in a client with Palliation-related concerns?
choices: Rapidly worsening vital signs or acute change in level of consciousness
Stable vital signs with normal mentation
A long-standing symptom that is unchanged from baseline
Mild discomfort that improves with rest
answer: Rapidly worsening vital signs or acute change in level of consciousness
rationale: Acute deterioration (vital signs/LOC) is a red flag requiring urgent escalation.

stem: [Palliation] The nurse is providing discharge teaching on Palliation. Which statement by the client shows correct understanding?
choices: I will follow the plan consistently and seek help early if symptoms worsen
I will avoid follow-up visits unless I have severe pain
I will change doses based on how I feel without contacting my provider
I will stop treatment as soon as I feel better
answer: I will follow the plan consistently and seek help early if symptoms worsen
rationale: Adherence and early follow-up reduce complications and relapse.

stem: [Palliation] A client is at risk for complications related to Palliation. Which assessment is most important to trend?
choices: Focused assessment plus objective measures (vitals, I&O, labs) relevant to the condition
Daily television viewing time
Hair distribution and nail length
Favorite foods and hobbies
answer: Focused assessment plus objective measures (vitals, I&O, labs) relevant to the condition
rationale: Trends in objective findings detect early deterioration and guide interventions.

stem: [Palliation] Which intervention best supports patient safety when caring for a client with Palliation concerns?
choices: Encourage the client to ambulate alone immediately regardless of status
Use standard precautions and implement condition-specific safety measures
Skip reassessment after interventions to reduce interruptions
Use room number as the only identifier before medications
answer: Use standard precautions and implement condition-specific safety measures
rationale: Safety includes infection control, reassessment, fall prevention, and correct identification.

stem: [Palliation] A client asks why a specific symptom occurs in Palliation. Which explanation is most appropriate?
choices: It happens randomly and cannot be explained
It always means the patient is exaggerating
It is caused only by dehydration in all cases
It reflects the underlying mechanism of the condition and the body’s compensatory response
answer: It reflects the underlying mechanism of the condition and the body’s compensatory response
rationale: Teaching should connect symptoms to mechanism and compensation in a clear, nonjudgmental way.

stem: [Palliation] Which delegation is appropriate when managing care related to Palliation?
choices: Delegate routine, stable tasks (e.g., vitals) to UAP and keep assessment/teaching/evaluation with the nurse
Delegate medication teaching to UAP
Delegate initial assessment of new chest pain to UAP
Delegate evaluation of response to a new IV medication to UAP
answer: Delegate routine, stable tasks (e.g., vitals) to UAP and keep assessment/teaching/evaluation with the nurse
rationale: Delegation must match scope: UAP can do routine tasks; nurse retains assessment/teaching/evaluation.

stem: [Palliation] The nurse suspects medication-related complication affecting Palliation. What is the best first step?
choices: Hold the suspected med if unsafe per parameters, assess, and notify provider per policy
Give an extra dose to overcome the problem
Ignore the finding if the client is not complaining
Stop all medications without notifying anyone
answer: Hold the suspected med if unsafe per parameters, assess, and notify provider per policy
rationale: Safety first: assess, follow parameters, and communicate changes promptly.

stem: [Palliation] Which documentation is most appropriate for a Palliation-related event?
choices: The patient was being difficult and noncompliant
The patient is dramatic and exaggerating
The caregiver is obviously lying
Objective findings with measurements and direct quotes
answer: Objective findings with measurements and direct quotes
rationale: Document objectively; avoid labels and conclusions.

stem: [Palliation] A client becomes anxious while discussing Palliation. Which nurse response is most therapeutic?
choices: Use calm presence and ask an open-ended question to explore concerns
Let’s not talk about it
Don’t worry—you’ll be fine
You shouldn’t feel that way
answer: Use calm presence and ask an open-ended question to explore concerns
rationale: Therapeutic communication uses empathy, calm presence, and open-ended questions.
